name: Umm Kulthum bint Ali
title: Umm Kulthum bint Ali (RA): Granddaughter of the Prophet
meta_description: The story of Umm Kulthum bint Ali (RA), granddaughter of the Prophet Muhammad and wife of Umar ibn al-Khattab. A life of quiet dignity, service, and patience through repeated loss.
keywords: Umm Kulthum bint Ali, daughter of Ali and Fatimah, wife of Umar ibn al-Khattab, granddaughter of the Prophet, ahl al-bayt, sahaba stories
lede: Late one night, on the edge of Madinah, a woman knelt in a worn-out tent and helped a frightened stranger give birth. Outside, her husband cooked over a fire he had built himself. The travellers had no idea who these two people were.

This is the story of Umm Kulthum bint Ali (may Allah be pleased with her): a granddaughter of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, the wife of a leader who ruled half the known world, and a woman who never once acted as though she were above anyone.

sections.h2: Born at the close of an era
paras: Umm Kulthum was the younger daughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah, the daughter of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Her sister Zaynab was older. She was born in the last days of the Prophet's life, so close to the end that she was almost certainly too young to ever remember him.

She grew up inside the household of the Prophet's family, with all its honour and all its sorrow. The blood of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ ran through her, and so did the weight of being born into a family that would be tested again and again.

She was a sister to Hasan and Husayn and to Zaynab, the grandchildren of the Prophet ﷺ. Look at the names that would pass through her life, and the trials that came with each of them, and you begin to see how much Allah placed in her path.

Her story, from its very beginning, is marked by loss. But there is something steady running underneath it, a quiet dignity that never bends, and that is what stays with you long after the names and dates fade.

sections.h2: A marriage that joined two houses
paras: When she reached the age of marriage, Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him) came to her father and asked for her hand. Ali raised two things: that there was a large age gap between them, and that he had been reserving his daughters for the sons of his brother Ja'far.

Umar would not let it rest. He said, "Let me marry her, for by Allah you will not find a man on the face of the earth who would care more for her companionship than I will." He was not only asking; he was promising to treat her well. Ali answered him: "Then let it be." And so she was married to Umar.

Umar was so moved that he went to the companions and said, "Congratulate me." They did not understand. People married all the time. But Umar had heard the Prophet ﷺ say that every tie and every lineage would be cut off on the Day of Judgment except his own. By marrying into the family of the Prophet ﷺ, Umar had drawn one more connection to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ that he hoped would not be severed on that day.

He made her dowry forty thousand to honour her lineage, and he meant it. This was the same Umar who would give the grandsons of the Prophet ﷺ, Hasan and Husayn, a share equal to their father's, even when the usual measures of rank did not apply. Into this house Umm Kulthum came as a young bride, and she would bear Umar two children, a son named Zayd and a daughter.

One of the most loved stories about Umar belongs, quietly, to her as well. As caliph, Umar would walk the city at night to check on his people. One night he came upon a battered tent on the outskirts of Madinah. A man sat outside it, clearly distressed, and from within came the sound of a woman in pain.

Umar asked what was wrong. The man, not knowing he was speaking to the caliph, told him to mind his own business. Umar pressed gently until the man finally explained: his wife was giving birth, alone, with no one to help her and no food, because they were travellers who knew no one in the city.

Umar went home. He said to his wife, "O daughter of the noble ones, would you like some good that Allah has driven your way?" She asked what it was. He told her of the woman in labour, and together they gathered flour and fat and what they needed to cook. Umar carried it on his own back.

Umm Kulthum went inside and helped the woman deliver her child, while Umar built a fire and cooked for the husband. When she called out, "O leader of the believers, tell him his wife has had a son," only then did the man realise who had been serving him through the night. Neither of them had ever felt above this. Not Umar with all his rank, not Umm Kulthum with all her lineage.

The same household held itself to a high line even in the smallest things. When the governor of a province sent Umar containers of honey and butter for the benefit of the Muslims, Umm Kulthum had quietly asked for two vessels of it. To anyone else this would have been nothing. She was the granddaughter of the Prophet ﷺ.

Umar noticed the two vessels were missing and asked about them. When he learned his own daughter-in-law had taken them, he had the traders estimate what had been used. She had taken only a little from the top of the honey. He paid for it, about three dirhams, from his own hand.

"We, the family of the Prophet ﷺ," the lesson runs, "carry a different level of dignity." It was not harshness, and it was not for show. It was simply a family that would not take what was not clearly theirs, even when the amount was tiny and no one would ever have noticed.

It is a striking pair of stories to hold together. The same woman who would kneel all night beside a stranger would also not keep three dirhams of honey that were not plainly hers. Generosity and scruple, both quiet, both running from the same heart.
When Umar was stabbed, Umm Kulthum was among those nursing his wounds. He did not recover. She became a young widow with two small children, and tragedy had visited her once again.

Following his own way of honouring his brother's house, Ali then married her to a son of Ja'far named Awn. Awn died. He married her to another son, Muhammad ibn Ja'far. He too died. She had now been widowed three times.

Her mother-in-law through these marriages was Asma bint Umays, whose sons these were. Umm Kulthum once said something that reveals her tenderness more than any grand speech could: "I feel shy before my mother-in-law. Two of her sons have died while married to me, and I am afraid the third will die with me as well." She carried not only her own grief, but a quiet care for the woman who had lost two sons to the same marriage.
But Allah decreed otherwise. It was she who would pass away while her last husband, Abdullah ibn Ja'far, still lived.

Her end came on a single night. A dispute broke out among one of the tribes, and her son Zayd, now grown, went out late to settle the fighting between them. In the middle of trying to make peace, he was struck on the head and died.

They came to her that night and told her that Zayd had gone out to solve people's problems, had stepped between those who were fighting, and had been killed. She began to weep, and she died that very same night. Another sorrow had passed through the household of the Prophet ﷺ.

The next morning, the funeral prayer was offered for mother and son together. Abdullah ibn Ja'far, who had by then become her husband, led the prayer over both. From this very event, some of the rulings on how to pray over a woman and a man at once were taken. They were buried together, the last of the children of Ali and Fatimah, may Allah be pleased with them all.

sections.h2: The man who learned the truth
paras: There is a final story worth telling. There was once a man who used to curse the companions of the Prophet ﷺ, doing so, he imagined, out of love for the family of the Prophet ﷺ. It is a contradiction at its heart: the family of the Prophet ﷺ loved the companions.
Someone walked up to him and said simply, "Do you realise that Ali married his daughter to Umar, and that Umar had his grandchildren?" The man said, "Really? I did not know that."

It changed him. He turned back to Allah, and it is said he spent the rest of his life seeking forgiveness, giving in charity, and weeping over every time he had let his tongue loose against the companions.

One small fact about this quiet woman was enough to soften a hardened heart. She had not argued with him or stood on a platform. The simple shape of her life, a granddaughter of the Prophet ﷺ married to Umar, was an answer all on its own.

lessons_heading: What Umm Kulthum's life teaches us
lessons_intro: Her name appears at the edge of famous stories, never at the centre of the noise. That is exactly where her example lives.

lessons.bold: Honour does not make you above service.
lessons.text: A granddaughter of the Prophet ﷺ knelt in a stranger's tent to help her give birth. Real standing shows itself in who you are willing to serve, not who you expect to serve you.

lessons.bold: Dignity holds the small things too.
lessons.text: Her household paid three dirhams for a little honey rather than take what was not clearly theirs. Integrity is most real when the amount is small and no one would notice.

lessons.bold: Patience is its own kind of strength.
lessons.text: She buried husband after husband and carried her grief quietly. She did not perform her sorrow; she lived through it with faith.

lessons.bold: A single truth can change a heart.
lessons.text: One fact about her marriage turned a man away from bitterness for the rest of his life. Speak the truth gently; you never know what it will mend.

faq.q: Who was Umm Kulthum bint Ali?
faq.a: She was the younger daughter of Ali ibn Abi Talib and Fatimah, the daughter of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, which made her a granddaughter of the Prophet. She later married Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with him).

faq.q: Why did Umar ibn al-Khattab marry her?
faq.a: Umar had heard the Prophet ﷺ say that every tie and lineage would be cut off on the Day of Judgment except his own. By marrying into the family of the Prophet ﷺ, Umar sought a lasting connection to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ, and he honoured her lineage with a generous dowry.

faq.q: How many times was Umm Kulthum widowed?
faq.a: After Umar was killed, her father married her to two sons of Ja'far, Awn and then Muhammad ibn Ja'far, both of whom died. She then married a third son, Abdullah ibn Ja'far, and it was she who passed away while he was still living.

faq.q: What can we learn from her life?
faq.a: That honour should never lift a person above serving others, that dignity holds firm even in the smallest matters, and that patience through repeated loss is a quiet and lasting kind of faith.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It would have been easy for Umm Kulthum to live behind her lineage. She was the granddaughter of the Prophet ﷺ and the wife of the most powerful man of her time. She could have expected the world to come to her. Instead she carried food on no one's back but her own household's, knelt beside a stranger in the dark, and held her many sorrows without complaint.

Most of us will be tested the way she was: not in front of crowds, but in ordinary days, in how we treat the people no one is watching us treat. Her life is a quiet invitation to keep our dignity small and unbroken, to serve without being asked, and to meet loss with patience rather than performance. Take one of those into today, and her story has done its work.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umm Kulthum bint Ali (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

Umar ibn al-Khattab asked to marry her.

"By Allah," he said, "you will not find a man who would care more for her companionship than I will."

He had heard the Prophet ﷺ say every lineage would be cut off on the Day of Judgment, except his own.

He wanted that one connection.

As caliph, Umar walked the city at night.

He found a woman giving birth alone in a tent, with no food and no help.

He carried the food home on his own back.

Umm Kulthum went inside and delivered the baby. Umar built the fire and cooked.

The travellers had no idea who they were.

She was widowed again and again.

Umar. Then Awn. Then Muhammad ibn Ja'far.

She buried them and carried her grief quietly, never performing her sorrow.

**Honour does not make you above service.**

A granddaughter of the Prophet ﷺ knelt in a stranger's tent in the dark.

May Allah be pleased with her.
social.caption:
She was the granddaughter of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ and the wife of Umar ibn al-Khattab (may Allah be pleased with them). Umm Kulthum bint Ali had every reason to expect the world to serve her.

Instead, on a cold night in Madinah, she knelt in a worn-out tent and helped a frightened stranger give birth, while her husband, the leader of the believers, cooked over a fire he had built himself. The travellers never knew who they were.

She was widowed more than once and carried her grief without complaint. Her life is a quiet lesson in dignity that never looks down on anyone.

Which part of her story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/umm-kulthum

email.subject: The granddaughter of the Prophet who served strangers in the dark
email.alt_subject: She knelt in a stranger's tent at night
email.preview: She was a granddaughter of the Prophet and the wife of a caliph, and she never acted above anyone.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Umm Kulthum bint Ali (may Allah be pleased with her) was the younger daughter of Ali and Fatimah, which made her a granddaughter of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She was born so close to the end of his life that she was almost certainly too young to remember him.

When she reached the age of marriage, Umar ibn al-Khattab asked for her hand. "By Allah," he said, "you will not find a man who would care more for her companionship than I will." He had heard the Prophet ﷺ say that every tie and lineage would be cut off on the Day of Judgment except his own, and he longed for that one connection to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ.

There is a story about her that is easy to love. As caliph, Umar would walk Madinah at night to check on his people. One night he found a woman giving birth alone in a battered tent, travellers with no food and no one to help them.

He went home. "O daughter of the noble ones," he said to Umm Kulthum, "would you like some good that Allah has driven your way?" Together they gathered flour and fat, and Umar carried it on his own back. She went inside and delivered the baby. He built a fire and cooked. The travellers had no idea they were being served by the leader of the believers and the granddaughter of the Prophet ﷺ.

Neither of them ever felt above this. Not Umar with all his rank, not Umm Kulthum with all her lineage.

Her life held real sorrow. She was widowed more than once and carried her grief quietly, without ever performing it. There is a lesson in her that is easy to miss: honour does not make you above service. The truly noble are the ones still willing to kneel in a stranger's tent in the dark.

May Allah be pleased with her, and soften our hearts to serve the people no one is watching us serve.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Late one night, on the edge of Madinah, a woman knelt in a worn-out tent and helped a frightened stranger give birth. Outside, her husband cooked over a fire he had built with his own hands. The travellers had no idea who these two people were.

Her name was Umm Kulthum bint Ali (may Allah be pleased with her), the younger daughter of Ali and Fatimah, and a granddaughter of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. She was born in the last days of his life, too young to ever remember him.

When she reached the age of marriage, Umar ibn al-Khattab asked for her hand. "By Allah," he said, "you will not find a man who would care more for her companionship than I will." He honoured her lineage, for he had heard the Prophet ﷺ say that every tie would be cut off on the Day of Judgment except his own, and he wanted that one connection.

As caliph, Umar walked the city at night. The night he found that tent, with a woman labouring alone and no food in sight, he went straight home. "O daughter of the noble ones," he said to his wife, "would you like some good that Allah has driven your way?" Together they carried flour and fat back through the dark.

She delivered the baby while Umar cooked. Only when she called out, "O leader of the believers, tell him his wife has had a son," did the husband realise who had been serving him all night. Neither of them had ever felt above it: not Umar with his rank, not Umm Kulthum with her blood.

Her life was marked by loss. She nursed Umar's wounds after he was stabbed and became a young widow. She was married again, and widowed again, and carried each grief quietly, never once performing her sorrow.

She died on a single night, weeping for her son Zayd, who had been killed trying to make peace between two quarrelling groups. The next morning, mother and son were prayed over together and buried side by side, the last of the children of Ali and Fatimah, may Allah be pleased with them all.
storybook.pullquote: Neither of them had ever felt above it: not Umar with his rank, not Umm Kulthum with her blood.
storybook.reflection: Umm Kulthum had every reason to expect the world to serve her, and instead she served a stranger in the dark. Her life is a quiet reminder that honour is never an excuse to look down on anyone, and that the noblest people are the ones still willing to kneel and help when no one is watching.
storybook.source: Her story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
